---
title: "Migration from ArviZ"
description: How to transition from imperative ArviZ approaches to declarative tidydraws workflows
---

If you currently wrangle MCMC output with raw ArviZ/xarray, this page shows what changes when you adopt `tidydraws`. The payoff is the same in every case: a tidy Polars frame that plugs straight into a plotting library. The plots below use lets-plot.

## Setup

```{python}
import numpy as np
import arviz as az
import polars as pl
import tidydraws as td
from xarray import DataArray
from lets_plot import *

LetsPlot.setup_html()
```

## The Problem: Manual xarray Wrangling

With raw ArviZ you access parameters by slicing xarray dimensions and reshaping by hand. This is imperative, verbose, and easy to get wrong.

```{python}
np.random.seed(42)
chains, draws = 4, 500
n_groups = 4

beta_np = np.random.normal(1.0, 0.2, (chains, draws, n_groups))
sigma_np = np.random.normal(0.8, 0.1, (chains, draws))

dt = az.from_dict(
    {
        "posterior": {
            "beta": DataArray(beta_np, dims=["chain", "draw", "groups"]),
            "sigma": DataArray(sigma_np, dims=["chain", "draw"]),
        }
    },
    coords={"groups": [f"g{i}" for i in range(n_groups)]},
    dims={"beta": ["groups"]},
)

# Manual: stack chain/draw, move groups to rows, build a DataFrame by hand
da = dt.posterior["beta"]
manual = pl.DataFrame(
    da.values.reshape(-1, n_groups), schema=[f"g{i}" for i in range(n_groups)]
).melt(variable_name="groups", value_name="beta")
manual.head()
```

That works, but every new variable or dimension means another reshape, and joining parameters to predictions is left entirely to you.

## The tidydraws Way: One Declarative Call

`spread_draws()` parses the string spec, infers coordinates, and returns a tidy `DataFrame` in one line.

```{python}
tidy = td.spread_draws(dt, "beta[groups]")
tidy.head()
```

Same data, no manual reshaping — and the frame is ready to plot.

```{python}
# | fig-cap: "Posterior density of `beta` by group — tidydraws output, lets-plot rendering."

(
    ggplot(tidy.to_pandas(), aes(x="beta", fill="groups"))
    + geom_density(alpha=0.5)
    + labs(x="beta", y="density", fill="group", title="Posterior beta by group")
    + theme_minimal()
)
```

## Prior vs. Posterior, Compared

Adding a prior group and comparing is a single `spread_draws_compare()` call — no manual concatenation.

```{python}
prior = az.from_dict(
    {
        "posterior": dt.children["posterior"].to_dataset(),
        "prior": {
            "beta": DataArray(
                np.random.normal(0.0, 0.5, (chains, draws, n_groups)),
                dims=["chain", "draw", "groups"],
            ),
            "sigma": DataArray(
                np.random.normal(1.0, 0.2, (chains, draws)), dims=["chain", "draw"]
            ),
        },
    },
    coords={"groups": [f"g{i}" for i in range(n_groups)]},
    dims={"beta": ["groups"]},
)

compare = td.spread_draws_compare(prior, "beta[groups]")
compare.head()
```

```{python}
# | fig-cap: "Prior vs. posterior of `beta` by group."

(
    ggplot(compare.to_pandas(), aes(x="beta", fill="source"))
    + geom_density(alpha=0.5)
    + facet_wrap(facets="groups", ncol=2)
    + labs(x="beta", y="density", fill="source", title="Prior vs. posterior by group")
    + theme_minimal()
)
```

## Prediction Space: No More Denormalised Tables

The classic ArviZ pitfall is building one giant "denormalised" table where every parameter is duplicated across every observation. `tidydraws` keeps parameter and prediction spaces separate.

```{python}
total_obs = 80
x_vals = np.random.normal(10, 5, total_obs)
group_assignments = np.random.randint(0, n_groups, total_obs)

mu = np.zeros((chains, draws, total_obs))
for i in range(total_obs):
    g = group_assignments[i]
    mu[:, :, i] = sigma_np[:, :] + beta_np[:, :, g] * x_vals[i]

pred_dt = az.from_dict(
    {
        "posterior": {
            "beta": DataArray(beta_np, dims=["chain", "draw", "groups"]),
            "sigma": DataArray(sigma_np, dims=["chain", "draw"]),
        },
        "predictions": {
            "mu": DataArray(mu, dims=["chain", "draw", "obs_ind"]),
        },
        "constant_data": {
            "x": DataArray(x_vals, dims=["obs_ind"]),
            "group": DataArray(group_assignments, dims=["obs_ind"]),
        },
    },
    coords={
        "groups": [f"g{i}" for i in range(n_groups)],
        "obs_ind": list(range(total_obs)),
    },
    dims={"beta": ["groups"], "x": ["obs_ind"], "group": ["obs_ind"]},
    pred_dims={"mu": ["obs_ind"]},
)

pred = td.add_epred_draws(
    pred_dt, newdata=None, var_name="mu", constant_data_group="constant_data"
)
pred.head()
```

Note the columns: `chain`, `draw`, `obs_ind`, `x`, `group`, `mu`. No `beta`, no `sigma` — parameters stay in their own frame.

```{python}
# | fig-cap: "Posterior predictive fit by group — tidydraws prediction frame, lets-plot rendering."

summary = (
    pred
    .group_by("obs_ind", "x", "group")
    .agg(
        pl.col("mu").quantile(0.055).alias("lower"),
        pl.col("mu").median().alias("median"),
        pl.col("mu").quantile(0.945).alias("upper"),
    )
    .sort("x")
)

(
    ggplot(summary.to_pandas(), aes(x="x"))
    + geom_ribbon(aes(ymin="lower", ymax="upper", fill="factor(group)"), alpha=0.25)
    + geom_line(aes(y="median", color="factor(group)"), size=0.8)
    + labs(
        x="x",
        y="mu",
        color="group",
        fill="group",
        title="Posterior predictive fit by group",
    )
    + theme_minimal()
)
```

## Migration Cheat Sheet

| Task | ArviZ (manual) | tidydraws |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Extract `beta[groups]` | `dt.posterior["beta"].values` + reshape | `spread_draws(dt, "beta[groups]")` |
| Compare prior/posterior | concat two arrays by hand | `spread_draws_compare(dt, "beta[groups]")` |
| Join predictions to covariates | manual merge on obs index | `add_epred_draws(dt, var_name="mu")` |
| Filter before plotting | slice after materialising | `.filter(...)` on the returned `DataFrame` |

## Summary

- Replace manual xarray slicing with declarative string specs.
- Parameter and prediction spaces stay separate — no denormalised tables.
- The eager `DataFrame` supports `.filter()` directly before plotting.
- The tidy output plugs straight into lets-plot (or any backend — see [Plotting Backends](05-backends.qmd)).
